level

To begin, we define a stored procedure in MySQL that safely handles the deletion of inactive users while logging their status changes. The procedure starts by declaring variables to capture error codes and messages for robust error handling. We then initiate a transaction to ensure data consistency across the deletion and logging operations. Before deleting any records, the procedure inserts a new entry into the status_log table for each user marked as Inactive. This log entry records the user_id, the previous status, and the current timestamp as the modification_date. Once the logging is complete, the procedure proceeds to delete the actual records from the users table where the status is Inactive. If any step within the transaction fails, the code immediately rolls back all changes to prevent partial updates. After a successful execution, the transaction is committed to permanently save the modifications. The procedure includes a specific handler to catch any SQL exceptions and return a clear error message to the caller. You can invoke this stored procedure whenever you need to clean up inactive user data from your database. The total count of affected rows will be returned to verify the operation was successful. This approach ensures that no data is lost without a traceable audit trail in the status_log table.
